nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: I’m Jocelyn, but everybody calls me Joss. She/her. I don’t really call myself butch or stud — masc presenting feels closer. People see a Black masc person and immediately go “stud,” but that never really fit me. I’ve always just kinda been me. Tomboy kid that never switched genres.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: “Switched genres” is kind of perfect. Did people push labels onto you before you even had language for yourself?

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: Constantly. Especially “stud.” And honestly, some of it felt racialized more than anything. Like people saw Black + masc and decided the rest for me.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: Do you feel like people want masc queer folks to perform a role for them?

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: Yeah. Like there’s this expectation that if you present masculine, you’re supposed to become “the man” in the relationship. Pay for everything. Open every door like you’re auditioning for a truck commercial.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: Truck commercial masculinity is honestly terrifying.

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: Exactly. I’ll open the door because I’m carrying groceries, not because gender commanded me to.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: It sounds like a lot of your identity has been about refusing scripts people tried to hand you.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: You mentioned your mom trying to make you more feminine growing up. Was that constant?

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: Oh absolutely. Dresses, makeup, all of it. One time I literally woke up and she was putting makeup on me before school because I refused to wear it willingly.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: That’s such an intimate level of “please become the version of you I understand.”

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: Yeah. I don’t even think she meant harm necessarily. I think she thought she was helping me survive the world better.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: Did leaving for Knoxville feel like escape or reinvention?

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: More like breathing room. Memphis felt too close to everybody’s expectations of me.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: Sometimes identity development is literally just getting far enough away to hear your own thoughts.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: What’s it like moving through Tennessee visibly masc-presenting?

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: You stay aware. Especially in smaller towns. And honestly being Black changes the equation too.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: Like scanning a room before your body even catches up?

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: Exactly. Bathrooms especially. People do the double-take thing constantly.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: That sounds exhausting over time.

nodes.speaker: J
nodes.text: It is. You get used to it, but “used to it” isn’t the same thing as okay.

nodes.speaker: N
nodes.text: That constant awareness feels like its own kind of labor people don’t always see.
